title: Learning from the Unseen: Generative Data Augmentation for Geometric-Semantic Accident Anticipation
summary: Anticipating traffic accidents is a critical yet unresolved problem for autonomous driving, hindered by the inherent complexity of modeling interactions between road users and the limited availability of diverse, large-scale datasets. To address these issues, we propose a dual-path framework. On the one hand, we employ a video synthesis pipeline that, guided by structured prompts, derives feature distributions from existing corpora and produces high-fidelity synthetic driving scenes consistent with the statistical patterns of real data. On the other hand, we design a graph neural network enriched with semantic cues, enabling dynamic reasoning over both spatial and semantic relations among participants. To validate the effectiveness of our approach, we release a new benchmark dataset containing standardized, finely annotated video sequences that cover a broad spectrum of regions, weather, and traffic conditions. Evaluations across existing datasets and our new benchmark confirm notable gains in both accuracy and anticipation lead time, highlighting the capacity of the proposed framework to mitigate current data bottlenecks and enhance the reliability of autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: A dual-path framework combining synthetic video generation and semantic graph neural networks to improve traffic accident anticipation for autonomous driving, validated on a new comprehensive benchmark dataset.
AI.motivation: Traffic accident anticipation is critical yet unresolved for autonomous driving due to the complexity of modeling road user interactions and the scarcity of diverse, large-scale datasets.
AI.method: (1) A video synthesis pipeline guided by structured prompts that generates high-fidelity synthetic driving scenes matching real data distributions. (2) A graph neural network enriched with semantic cues for dynamic spatial-semantic reasoning among traffic participants.
AI.result: Notable gains in both accuracy and anticipation lead time across existing datasets and the new benchmark, confirming the framework's ability to mitigate data bottlenecks and enhance system reliability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-path framework effectively addresses data scarcity and interaction modeling challenges, improving accident anticipation performance for autonomous driving.

title: Two-View Accumulation as the Primary Training Lever for Hybrid-Capture Gaussian Splatting: A Variance-Decomposition View of When Gradient Surgery Helps
summary:
Hybrid-capture novel view synthesis combines images at substantially different camera distances (e.g., aerial drone and ground-level views). Standard 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), trained for 30K iterations with one rendered view per optimizer step, under-fits the minority regime by 1-3 dB on five hybrid-capture benchmarks. We isolate the lever that closes this gap.
  Among compute-matched alternatives -- vanilla 60K iterations, magnitude corrections (GradNorm), direction-aware near/far gradient surgery, projective preconditioning, confidence-gated sample-level surgery, and a random two-view-per-step control -- the simplest structural change wins: rendering two views per optimizer step. The pairing rule (geometry-defined near/far, random, or active loss-disparity) does not change PSNR beyond seed variance on any of the five scenes; the structural change of having two views per step does.
  We propose a variance-decomposition framework that predicts and explains this finding: under bimodal camera regimes, between-regime gradient variance turns out to be small relative to within-regime variance in 3DGS, so structured and random pairings are variance-equivalent in expectation, and the variance halving from two-view accumulation itself is the dominant effect. We verify the framework on five scenes whose camera-altitude bimodality coefficients span [0.55, 1.00], and we report the negative result that direction-aware projection, magnitude correction, confidence gating, and an active loss-disparity pairing all fall within seed variance of random two-view pairing. The two-view structural lever transfers cleanly to the Scaffold-GS and Pixel-GS backbones.
  We position this work as an honest characterization of which training-side axes do and do not move PSNR for hybrid-capture 3DGS, together with the framework that explains why.

AI.tldr: For hybrid-capture novel view synthesis (aerial+ground), simply rendering two views per optimizer step significantly boosts 3DGS performance, while more complex methods (gradient surgery, GradNorm, confidence gating, etc.) do not improve beyond random two-view pairing. A variance-decomposition framework explains this: between-regime gradient variance is small relative to within-regime variance, making structured pairing unnecessary—the variance reduction from two-view accumulation itself is the dominant effect.
AI.motivation: Hybrid-capture novel view synthesis (e.g., combining aerial drone and ground-level views) suffers from under-fitting of the minority camera regime when using standard 3D Gaussian Splatting, losing 1-3 dB PSNR. The authors aim to identify which training-side modifications can close this performance gap.
AI.method: The authors systematically compare compute-matched alternatives: 60K iterations, GradNorm, near/far gradient surgery, projective preconditioning, confidence-gated surgery, random two-view-per-step, and geometry-defined near/far pairing. They also propose a variance-decomposition framework to theoretically explain why two-view accumulation works regardless of pairing strategy.
AI.result: The simplest change—rendering two views per optimizer step—closes the gap. Pairing rule (near/far, random, or active loss-disparity) does not affect PSNR beyond seed variance. Direction-aware projection, magnitude correction, confidence gating, and active loss-disparity pairing all fall within seed variance of random two-view pairing. The finding transfers to Scaffold-GS and Pixel-GS.
AI.conclusion: For hybrid-capture 3DGS, the dominant training-side lever is structural: two views per step. More complex gradient manipulation methods are unnecessary. The variance-decomposition framework explains why between-regime variance is small relative to within-regime variance, making any pairing strategy variance-equivalent.

title: AIDA-ReID: Adaptive Intermediate Domain Adaptation for Generalizable and Source-Free Person Re-Identification
summary: Person re-identification (Re-ID) aims to match images of the same individual across non-overlapping camera views and remains challenging due to domain shifts caused by variations in illumination, background, camera characteristics, and population distributions. Although supervised models perform well under matched training and testing conditions, their performance degrades significantly when deployed in unseen environments. Existing intermediate domain approaches such as IDM and IDM++ alleviate this gap by constructing bridge feature distributions between domains; however, they rely on fixed mixing strategies and joint source-target access, limiting their applicability to multi-source and source-free settings. To address these limitations, this paper proposes Adaptive Intermediate Domain Adaptation (AIDA), also referred to as Source-Free Multi-Source Intermediate Domain Adaptation (SF-MIDA). The proposed framework treats intermediate-domain learning as a dynamically regulated process, where feature mixing and regularization strength are adaptively controlled using feedback signals derived from model uncertainty and training stability. A multi-source intermediate domain generator synthesizes diverse intermediate representations, while a pseudo-mirror regularization strategy preserves identity consistency under domain perturbations. Extensive experiments across domain generalization and source-free settings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: AIDA (SF-MIDA) is a source-free, multi-source intermediate domain adaptation framework for person re-identification that dynamically controls feature mixing and regularization using uncertainty and stability feedback, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing intermediate domain methods (IDM, IDM++) rely on fixed mixing strategies and require joint source-target access, limiting their applicability in multi-source and source-free settings. This paper aims to overcome these limitations.
AI.method: Proposes Adaptive Intermediate Domain Adaptation (AIDA) with: (1) dynamically regulated feature mixing and regularization strength via model uncertainty and training stability feedback; (2) multi-source intermediate domain generator; (3) pseudo-mirror regularization for identity consistency under domain perturbations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across domain generalization and source-free settings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive intermediate domain approach successfully addresses fixed-mixing and joint-access limitations, offering a more flexible and robust solution for person Re-ID under domain shifts.

title: GAFSV-Net: A Vision Framework for Online Signature Verification
summary: Online signature verification (OSV) requires distinguishing skilled forgeries from genuine samples under high intra-class variability and with very few enrollment samples. Existing deep learning methods operate directly on raw temporal sequences, restricting them to 1D architectures and preventing the use of pretrained 2D vision backbones. We bridge this gap with GAFSV-Net, which represents each signature as a six-channel asymmetric Gramian Angular Field image: three kinematic channels (pen speed, pressure derivative, direction angle) are each encoded into complementary GASF and GADF matrices that capture pairwise temporal co-occurrence and directional transition structure respectively. A dual-branch ConvNeXt-Tiny encoder processes GASF and GADF independently, with bidirectional cross-attention enabling each branch to query discriminative patterns from the other before metric-space projection. Training uses semi-hard triplet loss with skilled-forgery hard-negative injection; verification is performed via cosine similarity against a small enrollment prototype. We evaluate on DeepSignDB and BiosecurID, outperforming all sequence-based baselines trained under identical objectives, demonstrating that the representational gain of 2D temporal encoding is consistent and independent of training procedure, with ablations characterising each design choice's contribution.

AI.tldr: GAFSV-Net converts online signature temporal sequences into 2D Gramian Angular Field images, enabling pretrained vision backbones (ConvNeXt-Tiny) for signature verification, outperforming sequence-based methods on DeepSignDB and BiosecurID.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for OSV operate on raw 1D temporal sequences, restricting them to 1D architectures and preventing the use of powerful pretrained 2D vision backbones.
AI.method: Each signature is encoded as a six-channel asymmetric Gramian Angular Field image from three kinematic channels (pen speed, pressure derivative, direction angle), processed by a dual-branch ConvNeXt-Tiny with bidirectional cross-attention, trained with semi-hard triplet loss and skilled-forgery hard-negative injection, verified via cosine similarity.
AI.result: Outperforms all sequence-based baselines under identical training objectives on DeepSignDB and BiosecurID; the representational gain is consistent and independent of training procedure.
AI.conclusion: 2D temporal encoding via GAF provides consistent representational advantages for OSV, enabling use of pretrained vision architectures and achieving state-of-the-art results.

authors: Udayanga G. W. K. N. Gamage
title: Real-Time Frame- and Event-based Object Detection with Spiking Neural Networks on Edge Neuromorphic Hardware: Design, Deployment and Benchmark
summary: Real-time object detection on energy-constrained platforms is critical for applications such as UAV-based inspection, autonomous navigation, and mobile robotics. Spiking neural networks (SNNs) on neuromorphic hardware are believed to be significantly more energy-efficient than conventional artificial neural networks (ANNs). In this work, we present a comprehensive methodology for designing general SNN detection architectures targeting neuromorphic platforms, along with the engineering adaptations required to deploy them on the state-of-the-art Neuromorphic processor, Intel Loihi 2. We benchmark SNN-based object detection on Loihi 2 using both frame-based and event-based datasets, comparing performance with ANN-based detection on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, NVIDIA Jetson Nano B01, and the Apple M2 CPU. Our results show that SNNs on Loihi 2 can perform real-time detection while achieving the lowest per-inference dynamic energy among all platforms. Also, Loihi 2 outperforms the other platforms in terms of power consumption, though ANNs on Jetson Orin Nano achieve higher inference rates. Furthermore, our ANN-to-SNN distillation-aware training enables SNNs to recover 87-100% of the detection accuracy of their ANN counterparts while maintaining lower inference latency; without distillation, SNNs exhibit an 11-27% accuracy drop. These results highlight the potential of neuromorphic systems for energy-efficient, real-time object detection at the edge.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a methodology for designing SNN-based object detectors on Intel Loihi 2, achieving real-time detection with the lowest per-inference dynamic energy compared to ANNs on Jetson Orin Nano, Jetson Nano B01, and Apple M2 CPU. Using ANN-to-SNN distillation-aware training, SNNs recover 87-100% of ANN accuracy, avoiding the 11-27% accuracy drop seen without distillation.
AI.motivation: Real-time object detection is critical for energy-constrained applications like UAVs and mobile robotics, and while SNNs on neuromorphic hardware promise better energy efficiency than conventional ANNs, there is a lack of comprehensive methodology and engineering adaptations for deploying general SNN detection architectures on neuromorphic processors like Loihi 2.
AI.method: The authors developed a general methodology for designing SNN detection architectures for neuromorphic platforms, applied engineering adaptations for deployment on Intel Loihi 2, and used ANN-to-SNN distillation-aware training to improve accuracy. They benchmarked on both frame-based and event-based datasets against ANN baselines on Jetson Orin Nano, Jetson Nano B01, and Apple M2 CPU.
AI.result: SNNs on Loihi 2 achieved the lowest per-inference dynamic energy and power consumption among all platforms, with real-time detection capability. ANN-to-SNN distillation recovered 87-100% of ANN accuracy; without distillation, SNNs suffered 11-27% accuracy drop. However, ANNs on Jetson Orin Nano achieved higher inference rates.
AI.conclusion: Neuromorphic systems, specifically SNNs on Loihi 2, show strong potential for energy-efficient, real-time object detection at the edge, offering substantial energy savings over ANNs while recovering near-full detection accuracy through distillation-aware training.

title: From Images2Mesh: A 3D Surface Reconstruction Pipeline for Non-Cooperative Space Objects
summary: On-orbit inspection imagery is crucial as it enables characterization of non-cooperative resident space objects, providing the geometry and structural condition essential for active debris removal and on-orbit servicing mission planning. However, most existing neural implicit surface reconstruction methods have been confined to synthetic or hardware-in-the-loop data with known camera poses and controlled illumination. In this work, we present a pipeline for neural implicit surface reconstruction of non-cooperative space objects from monocular inspection imagery. We demonstrate it on publicly released ISS inspection footage from the STS-119 mission and publicly released on-orbit inspection footage of an H-IIA rocket upper stage. We find that segmentation-based background removal is essential for successful camera pose estimation from real on-orbit footage, where background variation between frames caused direct processing to fail entirely. We further incorporate photometric correction of per-frame exposure variations and analyze its behavior across datasets, finding that performance in shadowed regions varies with the illumination characteristics of the input footage.

AI.tldr: A pipeline for neural implicit surface reconstruction of non-cooperative space objects from real monocular on-orbit inspection imagery, demonstrating that segmentation-based background removal and photometric correction are essential for successful 3D reconstruction from real-world space footage.
AI.motivation: Existing neural implicit surface reconstruction methods are limited to synthetic or controlled data; real on-orbit inspection footage of non-cooperative space objects (e.g., debris) remains unaddressed, hindering mission planning for active debris removal and on-orbit servicing.
AI.method: A pipeline using segmentation-based background removal for camera pose estimation, followed by neural implicit surface reconstruction with photometric correction for per-frame exposure variations, tested on real ISS inspection footage (STS-119) and H-IIA rocket upper stage footage.
AI.result: Segmentation-based background removal is essential for camera pose estimation; photometric correction improves performance but its effectiveness in shadowed regions depends on illumination characteristics of the input footage.
AI.conclusion: Neural implicit surface reconstruction can be successfully applied to real on-orbit inspection imagery with appropriate preprocessing (background removal, photometric correction), enabling 3D characterization of non-cooperative space objects from monocular video.

title: VkSplat: High-Performance 3DGS Training in Vulkan Compute
summary: We present VkSplat, a high-performance, cross-vendor 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) training pipeline implemented fully in Vulkan compute, addressing performance and compatibility limitation of existing training pipelines. With various optimizations, we achieve $3.3\times$ speed and $33\%$ VRAM reduction over CUDA+PyTorch baseline, maintaining quality, and demonstrating compatibility across GPU vendors. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first fully-Vulkan-based 3DGS training pipeline that achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code: \href{https://github.com/harry7557558/vksplat}{https://github.com/harry7557558/vksplat}

AI.tldr: VkSplat is the first fully Vulkan-based 3D Gaussian Splatting training pipeline that achieves 3.3x speedup and 33% VRAM reduction over CUDA+PyTorch baselines while maintaining cross-vendor GPU compatibility.
AI.motivation: To address performance and compatibility limitations of existing 3DGS training pipelines, which are typically tied to CUDA and specific GPU vendors.
AI.method: A fully Vulkan compute-based training pipeline for 3D Gaussian Splatting with various optimizations, implemented entirely in Vulkan without relying on CUDA or PyTorch.
AI.result: 3.3x faster training speed and 33% reduction in VRAM usage compared to the CUDA+PyTorch baseline, while maintaining output quality and demonstrating cross-vendor GPU compatibility.
AI.conclusion: VkSplat achieves state-of-the-art performance as the first fully Vulkan-based 3DGS training pipeline, offering a more portable and efficient alternative to CUDA-dependent implementations.

title: Adaptive Geodesic Conformal Prediction for Egocentric Camera Pose Estimation
summary: Egocentric pose estimation for Augmented Reality (AR) and assistive devices requires not just accurate predictions but guaranteed uncertainty regions. Conformal prediction (CP) provides such guarantees without retraining, but we show that standard CP with a single fixed threshold achieves nominal 90% overall coverage while covering only ~60% of the hardest 25% of frames (Q4) -- a ~30 percentage-point conditional coverage gap consistent across 12 participants, 3 predictors, and 3 horizons (108 evaluations) on EPIC-Fields. We further show that a geodesic SE(3) nonconformity score identifies physically harder frames than Euclidean scoring, with only 15-26% Q4 overlap and 2-3x higher ground-truth camera displacement for geodesic Q4 frames. To close the coverage gap, we propose DINOv2-Bridge adaptive CP: a two-stage difficulty estimator trained on a single source participant that transfers cross-participant without any images at test time, improving Q4 coverage from ~0.75 to ~0.93 while maintaining overall coverage at the 90% target.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a conditional coverage gap (30pp) in standard conformal prediction for egocentric pose estimation, proposes a geodesic SE(3) nonconformity score, and introduces DINOv2-Bridge adaptive CP to close the gap, improving Q4 coverage from ~0.75 to ~0.93 at 90% target.
AI.motivation: Standard conformal prediction for egocentric pose estimation achieves nominal coverage overall but fails on hard frames (Q4 coverage ~60% vs 90% target), creating a conditional coverage gap of ~30pp that persists across 108 evaluations.
AI.method: Proposes a geodesic SE(3) nonconformity score that identifies physically harder frames than Euclidean scoring, and introduces DINOv2-Bridge adaptive CP—a two-stage difficulty estimator trained on a single source participant that transfers cross-participant without any images at test time.
AI.result: Geodesic Q4 frames show only 15-26% overlap with Euclidean Q4 and 2-3x higher ground-truth camera displacement. DINOv2-Bridge improves Q4 coverage from ~0.75 to ~0.93 while maintaining 90% overall coverage.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive conformal prediction with a difficulty estimator that generalizes across participants can effectively close the conditional coverage gap for egocentric pose estimation without requiring test-time images.

title: MAEPose: Self-Supervised Spatiotemporal Learning for Human Pose Estimation on mmWave Video
summary: Millimetre-wave (mmWave) radar offers a more privacy-preserving alternative to RGB-based human pose estimation. However, existing methods typically rely on pre-extracted intermediate representations such as sparse point clouds or spectrogram images, where the rich spatiotemporal information naturally present in radar video streams is discarded for model learning, while such signal processing adds system complexity. In addition, existing solutions are mainly conducted in an end-to-end supervised manner without leveraging unlabelled raw video streams to learn generalized representations. In this study, we present MAEPose, a masked autoencoding-based human pose estimation approach that operates directly on mmWave spectrogram videos. MAEPose learns spatiotemporal motion-aware generalized representations from unlabelled radar video, and leverages its heatmap decoder for multi-frame pose estimation predictions. We evaluate it across three datasets based on leave-one-person-out cross-validation with rigorous statistical testing. MAEPose consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by up to 22.1% in MPJPE p<0.05, and maintains robust accuracy under zero-shot bystander interference with only a 6.5% error increase. Ablation studies confirm that both the pre-training and the heatmap decoder contribute substantially, while modality analysis indicates that leveraging Range-Doppler video as input achieves better pose estimation performance than Range-Azimuth or their fusion, with lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: MAEPose uses masked autoencoding on raw mmWave radar spectrogram videos for human pose estimation, outperforming baselines by up to 22.1% in MPJPE without relying on pre-extracted intermediate representations.
AI.motivation: Existing mmWave radar-based pose estimation methods discard rich spatiotemporal information by using pre-extracted intermediate representations (point clouds/spectrograms), and lack ability to leverage unlabelled raw video streams for learning generalized representations.
AI.method: MAEPose applies masked autoencoding directly on mmWave spectrogram videos to learn spatiotemporal motion-aware representations from unlabelled radar video, then uses a heatmap decoder for multi-frame pose estimation.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by up to 22.1% in MPJPE (p<0.05) across three datasets, maintains robust accuracy under zero-shot bystander interference (only 6.5% error increase), and Range-Doppler video input achieves best performance with lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: MAEPose demonstrates that direct learning from raw radar spectrogram videos via masked autoencoding is effective for privacy-preserving human pose estimation, with both pre-training and heatmap decoder design contributing substantially to performance.

title: Remote SAMsing: From Segment Anything to Segment Everything
summary: SAM2 produces high-quality zero-shot segmentation on natural images, but applying it to large remote sensing scenes exposes two problems: (1) its mask generator faces an inherent quality-coverage trade-off: strict thresholds yield precise masks but leave most of the image unsegmented, while relaxed thresholds increase coverage at the cost of mask quality; and (2) large images must be tiled, fragmenting objects across tile boundaries. We propose Remote SAMsing, an open-source pipeline that solves both problems without modifying SAM2 or requiring training data. For coverage, a multi-pass algorithm runs SAM2 repeatedly on each tile, painting accepted masks black between passes to simplify the scene for the next iteration, and relaxing quality thresholds only when coverage gains stagnate, ensuring that the most precise masks are always captured first. For spatial consistency, contextual padding and a parameter-free best-match merge reconstruct objects fragmented across tile boundaries. Evaluated on seven scenes (5~cm to 4.78~m GSD), the pipeline raises coverage from 30--68\% (single-pass SAM2) to 91--98\%. Ablation experiments quantify the contribution of each component to coverage and detection quality. Per-class evaluation shows that SAM2 transfers well to discrete RS objects (buildings 95\%, cars 82--93\% Det@0.5) with segment boundaries 3--8$\times$ more precise than SLIC and Felzenszwalb baselines. Tile size functions as an implicit scale parameter: reducing it from $1{,}000$ to 250 raises Det@0.5 from 56\% to 85\%, outperforming SAM2's built-in multi-scale mechanism. The pipeline generalizes to MNF false-color imagery without retraining (99.5\% ASA) and scales to production-sized images: a 1.94 billion pixel Potsdam mosaic achieved 97\% coverage without quality degradation.

AI.tldr: Remote SAMsing is an open-source pipeline that enhances SAM2 for remote sensing by using a multi-pass algorithm to improve segmentation coverage from 30-68% to 91-98%, and fixing object fragmentation across tile boundaries via contextual padding and merge techniques, without modifying SAM2 or requiring training data.
AI.motivation: Applying SAM2's zero-shot segmentation to large remote sensing scenes suffers from two problems: (1) a quality-coverage trade-off where strict thresholds give precise masks but poor coverage, and (2) large images must be tiled, which fragments objects across tile boundaries.
AI.method: Remote SAMsing uses a multi-pass algorithm on each tile: painting accepted masks black between passes to simplify the scene, relaxing quality thresholds only when coverage gains stagnate. Contextual padding and a parameter-free best-match merge reconstruct objects fragmented across tile boundaries.
AI.result: Coverage raised from 30-68% (single-pass SAM2) to 91-98% across seven scenes. Per-class: buildings 95%, cars 82-93% Det@0.5. Segment boundaries 3-8x more precise than SLIC/Felzenszwalb baselines. Scaling to 1.94 billion pixel mosaic achieved 97% coverage. Generalizes to MNF false-color imagery (99.5% ASA).
AI.conclusion: The pipeline successfully solves SAM2's quality-coverage trade-off and tile fragmentation issues for remote sensing, achieving high coverage and detection quality without modification or retraining, and scales to production-sized imagery.

title: REALM: An RGB and Event Aligned Latent Manifold for Cross-Modal Perception
summary: Event cameras provide several unique advantages over standard frame-based sensors, including high temporal resolution, low latency, and robustness to extreme lighting. However, existing learning-based approaches for event processing are typically confined to narrow, task-specific silos and lack the ability to generalize across modalities. We address this gap with REALM, a cross-modal framework that learns an RGB and Event Aligned Latent Manifold by projecting event representations into the pretrained latent space of RGB foundation models. Instead of task-specific training, we leverage low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to bridge the modality gap, effectively unlocking the geometric and semantic priors of frozen RGB backbones for asynchronous event streams. We demonstrate that REALM effectively maps events into the ViT-based foundation latent space. Our method allows us to perform downstream tasks like depth estimation and semantic segmentation by simply transferring linear heads trained on the RGB teacher. Most significantly, REALM enables the direct, zero-shot application of complex, frozen image-trained decoders, such as MASt3R, to raw event data. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in wide-baseline feature matching, significantly outperforming specialized architectures. Code and models are available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: REALM is a cross-modal framework that aligns event camera data with RGB foundation model latent spaces using LoRA, enabling zero-shot transfer of pretrained image decoders (like MASt3R) to event streams for tasks like depth estimation, semantic segmentation, and feature matching.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-based event processing methods are task-specific and cannot generalize across modalities, lacking the rich geometric and semantic priors available in RGB foundation models.
AI.method: REALM projects event representations into the pretrained latent space of RGB foundation models (ViT-based) using low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to bridge the modality gap, enabling frozen RGB backbones to process asynchronous event streams.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in wide-baseline feature matching, enables zero-shot application of complex image-trained decoders (MASt3R) to raw event data, and allows downstream tasks by transferring linear heads from RGB teachers.
AI.conclusion: The cross-modal alignment approach effectively unlocks pretrained RGB knowledge for event data, generalizing beyond task-specific silos and outperforming specialized architectures.

title: When Do Diffusion Models learn to Generate Multiple Objects?
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models achieve impressive visual fidelity, yet they remain unreliable in multi-object generation. Despite extensive empirical evidence of these failures, the underlying causes remain unclear. We begin by asking how much of this limitation arises from the data itself. To disentangle data effects, we consider two regimes across different dataset sizes: (1) concept generalization, where each individual concept is observed during training under potentially imbalanced data distributions, and (2) compositional generalization, where specific combinations of concepts are systematically held out. To study these regimes, we introduce mosaic (Multi-Object Spatial relations, AttrIbution, Counting), a controlled framework for dataset generation. By training diffusion models on mosaic, we find that scene complexity plays a dominant role rather than concept imbalance, and that counting is uniquely difficult to learn in low-data regimes. Moreover, compositional generalization collapses as more concept combinations are held out during training. These findings highlight fundamental limitations of diffusion models and motivate stronger inductive biases and data design for robust multi-object compositional generation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates why text-to-image diffusion models struggle with multi-object generation, finding through a controlled dataset framework (MOSAIC) that scene complexity, not concept imbalance, is the dominant limitation—with counting being especially hard and compositional generalization collapsing when concept combinations are held out during training.
AI.motivation: To uncover the root causes of diffusion models' unreliable multi-object generation by disentangling data-related factors (concept imbalance vs. compositional generalization) using a controlled dataset framework.
AI.method: The authors introduce MOSAIC (Multi-Object Spatial relations, Attribution, Counting), a controlled framework for generating datasets with specific properties. They train diffusion models on MOSAIC across two regimes: (1) concept generalization (imbalanced data distributions) and (2) compositional generalization (systematically held-out concept combinations), varying dataset sizes.
AI.result: Scene complexity dominates over concept imbalance as a limiting factor; counting is uniquely difficult in low-data regimes; compositional generalization degrades significantly as more concept combinations are held out during training.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models have fundamental limitations in multi-object compositional generation, motivating the need for stronger inductive biases and careful data design to achieve robust performance.

title: An End-to-End Decision-Aware Multi-Scale Attention-Based Model for Explainable Autonomous Driving
summary: The application of computer vision is gradually increasing across various domains. They employ deep learning models with a black-box nature. Without the ability to explain the behavior of neural networks, especially their decision-making processes, it is not possible to recognize their efficiency, predict system failures, or effectively implement them in real-world applications. Due to the inevitable use of deep learning in fully automated driving systems, many methods have been proposed to explain their behavior; however, they suffer from flawed reasoning and unreliable metrics, which have prevented a comprehensive understanding of complex models in autonomous vehicles and hindered the development of truly reliable systems. In this study, we propose a multi-scale attention-based model in which driving decisions are fed into the reasoning component to provide case-specific explanations for each decision simultaneously. For quantitative evaluation of our model's performance, we employ the F1-score metric, and also proposed a new metric called the Joint F1 score to demonstrate the accurate and reliable performance of the model in terms of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI). In addition to the BDD-OIA dataset, the nu-AR dataset is utilized to further validate the generalization capability and robustness of the proposed network. The results demonstrate the superiority of our reasoning network over the classic and state-of-the-art models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-scale attention-based model for explainable AI in autonomous driving, introducing a new Joint F1 score metric to evaluate explanation quality, and demonstrates superior performance on BDD-OIA and nu-AR datasets.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models in autonomous driving are black-box systems with unreliable explanations, hindering trust and real-world deployment. Existing explainability methods suffer from flawed reasoning and unreliable metrics.
AI.method: A multi-scale attention-based model where driving decisions feed into a reasoning component for case-specific explanations. Uses F1-score and a proposed Joint F1 score metric for XAI evaluation.
AI.result: The proposed model outperforms classic and state-of-the-art models on both BDD-OIA and nu-AR datasets, demonstrating superior generalization and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-scale attention-based reasoning network offers more reliable and accurate explainability for autonomous driving systems compared to existing methods.

title: Efficient Spatio-Temporal Vegetation Pixel Classification with Vision Transformers
summary: Plant phenology-the study of recurrent life cycle events-is essential for understanding ecosystem dynamics and their responses to climate change impacts. While Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) and near-surface cameras enable high-resolution monitoring, identifying plant species across time remains computationally challenging. State-of-the-art approaches, specifically Multi-Temporal Convolutional Networks (CNNs), rely on rigid multi-branch architectures that scale poorly with longer time series and require large spatial context windows. In this paper, we present an extensive study on optimizing Vision Transformers (ViTs) for efficient spatio-temporal vegetation pixel classification. We conducted a comprehensive ablation study analyzing seven key design dimensions, including: (i) data normalization; (ii) spectral arrangement; (iii) boundary handling; (iv) spatial context window shape and size; (v) tokenization strategies; (vi) positional encoding; and (vii) feature aggregation strategies. Our method was evaluated on two datasets from the Brazilian Cerrado biome, Serra do Cipó (aerial imagery) and Itirapina (near-surface imagery). Experimental results demonstrate that our ViT approach offers a substantial improvement in computational efficiency while maintaining competitive classification performance. Notably, our ViT reduces Floating Point Operations (FLOPs) by an order of magnitude and maintains constant parameter complexity regardless of the time series length, whereas the CNN baseline scales linearly. Our findings confirm that ViTs are a robust, scalable solution for resource-constrained phenological monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Vision Transformers (ViTs) as a more efficient and scalable alternative to Multi-Temporal CNNs for vegetation phenology classification, achieving competitive accuracy with an order of magnitude fewer FLOPs and constant parameter complexity regardless of time series length.
AI.motivation: Existing Multi-Temporal CNN approaches for phenological monitoring suffer from rigid multi-branch architectures that scale poorly with longer time series and require large spatial context windows, making them computationally expensive and unsuitable for resource-constrained systems.
AI.method: The authors conduct a comprehensive ablation study on seven key design dimensions of Vision Transformers (ViTs) for spatio-temporal vegetation pixel classification, including data normalization, spectral arrangement, boundary handling, spatial context window, tokenization, positional encoding, and feature aggregation. The method is evaluated on two Brazilian Cerrado biome datasets.
AI.result: ViTs reduce FLOPs by an order of magnitude compared to CNN baselines while maintaining competitive classification performance. ViTs also maintain constant parameter complexity regardless of time series length, whereas CNN baselines scale linearly.
AI.conclusion: Vision Transformers are a robust, scalable solution for resource-constrained phenological monitoring systems, offering substantial computational efficiency improvements over traditional multi-branch CNN architectures.

title: Beyond Visual Fidelity: Benchmarking Super-Resolution Models for Large-Scale Remote Sensing Imagery via Downstream Task Integration
summary: Super-resolution (SR) techniques have made major advances in reconstructing high-resolution images from low-resolution inputs. The increased resolution provides visual enhancement and utility for monitoring tasks. In particular, SR has been increasingly developed for satellite-based Earth observation, with applications in urban planning, agriculture, ecology, and disaster response. However, existing SR studies and benchmarks typically use fidelity metrics such as PSNR or SSIM, whereas the true utility of super-resolved images lies in supporting downstream tasks such as land cover classification, biomass estimation, and change detection. To bridge this gap, we introduce GeoSR-Bench, a downstream task-integrated SR benchmark dataset to evaluate SR models beyond fidelity metrics. GeoSR-Bench comprises spatially co-located, temporally aligned, and quality-controlled image pairs from about 36,000 locations across diverse land covers, spanning resolutions from 500m to 0.6m. To the best of our knowledge, GeoSR-Bench is the first SR benchmark that directly connects improved image resolution from SR models with downstream Earth monitoring tasks, including land cover segmentation, infrastructure mapping, and biophysical variable estimation. Using GeoSR-Bench, we benchmark GAN, transformer, neural operator, and diffusion-based SR models on perceptual quality and downstream task performance. We conduct experiments with 270 settings, covering 2 cross-platform SR tasks, 9 SR models, 3 downstream task models, and 5 downstream tasks for each SR task. The results show that improvements in traditional SR metrics often do not correlate with gains in task performance, and the correlations can be negative, indicating that these metrics provide limited guidance for selecting superior models for downstream tasks. This reveals the need to integrate downstream tasks into SR model development and evaluation.

AI.tldr: GeoSR-Bench is the first downstream task-integrated SR benchmark for satellite Earth observation, evaluating 9 SR models across 270 settings on 5 downstream tasks. Results show traditional fidelity metrics (PSNR/SSIM) often negatively correlate with task performance, highlighting the need for task-aware SR evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing SR benchmarks use fidelity metrics (PSNR, SSIM) that may not reflect real-world utility for downstream Earth monitoring tasks like land cover classification, biomass estimation, and change detection.
AI.method: GeoSR-Bench provides spatially co-located, temporally aligned, quality-controlled image pairs from ~36,000 locations across diverse land covers (500m to 0.6m resolution). It benchmarks GAN, transformer, neural operator, and diffusion-based SR models across 270 settings, covering 2 cross-platform SR tasks, 9 SR models, 3 downstream models, and 5 downstream tasks.
AI.result: Improvements in traditional SR metrics (PSNR, SSIM) often do not correlate with downstream task performance gains, and correlations can be negative, showing limited guidance for model selection.
AI.conclusion: Downstream tasks should be integrated into SR model development and evaluation, as fidelity metrics alone are insufficient for assessing real-world utility in Earth observation.

title: Online Self-Calibration Against Hallucination in Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) often suffer from hallucinations, generating descriptions that include visual details absent from the input image. Recent preference alignment methods typically rely on supervision distilled from stronger models such as GPT. However, this offline paradigm introduces a Supervision-Perception Mismatch: the student model is forced to align with fine-grained details beyond its perceptual capacity, learning to guess rather than to see. To obtain reliable self-supervision for online learning, we identify a Generative-Discriminative Gap within LVLMs, where models exhibit higher accuracy on discriminative verification than open-ended generation. Leveraging this capability, we propose \textbf{O}nline \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{CA}lib\textbf{R}ation (OSCAR), a framework that integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search with a Dual-Granularity Reward Mechanism to construct preference data and iteratively refines the model via Direct Preference Optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OSCAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on hallucination benchmarks while improving general multimodal capabilities.

AI.tldr: OSCAR is an online self-calibration framework that uses Monte Carlo Tree Search and dual-granularity rewards to reduce hallucinations in LVLMs by leveraging the model's own discriminative abilities, avoiding reliance on supervision from stronger models.
AI.motivation: LVLMs hallucinate due to a Supervision-Perception Mismatch when aligning with fine-grained details from stronger models that exceed the student model's perceptual capacity.
AI.method: OSCAR integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search with a Dual-Granularity Reward Mechanism to construct preference data, then iteratively refines the model via Direct Preference Optimization, exploiting the Generative-Discriminative Gap where models are better at verification than generation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on hallucination benchmarks while also improving general multimodal capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Online self-supervision (OSCAR) effectively reduces hallucinations and enhances LVLMs without relying on external stronger models, addressing the supervision-perception mismatch.

title: Pose-Aware Diffusion for 3D Generation
summary: Generating pose-aligned 3D objects is challenging due to the spatial mismatches and transformation ambiguities inherent in decoupled canonical-then-rotate paradigms. To this end, we introduce Pose-Aware Diffusion (PAD), a novel end-to-end diffusion framework that synthesizes 3D geometry directly within the observation space. By unprojecting monocular depth into a partial point cloud and explicitly injecting it as a 3D geometric anchor, PAD abandons canonical assumptions to enforce rigorous spatial supervision. This native generation intrinsically resolves pose ambiguity, producing high-fidelity pose-aligned assets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PAD achieves superior geometric alignment and image-to-3D correspondence compared to state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, PAD naturally extends to compositional 3D scene reconstruction via a simple union of independently generated objects, highlighting its robust ability to preserve precise spatial layouts.

AI.tldr: PAD is an end-to-end diffusion framework that generates pose-aligned 3D objects directly in observation space by unprojecting monocular depth into point clouds, avoiding canonical-then-rotate paradigms and achieving superior geometric alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing decoupled canonical-then-rotate paradigms suffer from spatial mismatches and transformation ambiguities when generating pose-aligned 3D objects.
AI.method: PAD unprojects monocular depth into a partial point cloud as a 3D geometric anchor, injects it into a diffusion framework, and synthesizes 3D geometry directly in observation space without canonical assumptions.
AI.result: PAD achieves superior geometric alignment and image-to-3D correspondence over state-of-the-art methods, and extends naturally to compositional 3D scene reconstruction while preserving spatial layouts.
AI.conclusion: The pose-aware native generation paradigm effectively resolves pose ambiguity and produces high-fidelity pose-aligned 3D assets, with robust spatial layout preservation for multi-object scenes.

title: CURE-OOD: Benchmarking Out-of-Distribution Detection for Survival Prediction
summary: ``How long can I live and remain free of cancer?'' is often the first question a patient asks after receiving a cancer diagnosis and treatment. Accurate survival prediction helps alleviate psychological distress and supports risk stratification and personalized treatment planning. Recent survival prediction frameworks have shown strong performance using computed tomography (CT) images. However, variations in imaging acquisition introduce out-of-distribution (OOD) samples caused by covariate shifts that undermine model reliability. Despite this challenge, to our knowledge, no existing benchmark systematically studies OOD detection in cancer survival prediction. To address this gap, we introduce the Cancer sURvival bEnchmark for OOD Detection (CURE-OOD), the first benchmark for systematically evaluating OOD detection in survival prediction under controlled acquisition-induced distribution shifts. CURE-OOD defines scanner-parameter-based training, in-distribution (ID), and OOD test splits across four survival prediction tasks. Our experiments show that covariate shifts notably reduce survival prediction performance. It also shows that mainstream classification-oriented OOD detectors can fail in survival prediction. Finally, we include HazardDev as a simple survival-aware reference baseline for OOD detection. CURE-OOD enables systematic analysis of how distribution shifts affect both downstream survival performance and OOD detectability.

AI.tldr: CURE-OOD is the first benchmark for evaluating out-of-distribution detection in cancer survival prediction from CT images under acquisition-induced covariate shifts, revealing that such shifts degrade performance and that standard OOD detectors often fail in this setting.
AI.motivation: Patients need accurate survival prediction after cancer diagnosis, but CT image acquisition variations cause covariate shifts that undermine model reliability. No existing benchmark systematically studies OOD detection in cancer survival prediction.
AI.method: The authors introduce CURE-OOD, a benchmark defining scanner-parameter-based training, ID, and OOD test splits across four survival prediction tasks. They evaluate survival prediction performance under covariate shifts and test mainstream classification-oriented OOD detectors in this context, including a simple survival-aware baseline called HazardDev.
AI.result: Covariate shifts notably reduce survival prediction performance. Mainstream classification-oriented OOD detectors can fail in survival prediction tasks.
AI.conclusion: CURE-OOD enables systematic analysis of how distribution shifts affect both downstream survival performance and OOD detectability, highlighting the need for survival-specific OOD detection methods.

title: Time-series Meets Complex Motion Modeling: Robust and Computational-effective Motion Predictor for Multi-object Tracking
summary: Multi-object tracking (MOT) is critical in numerous real-world applications, including surveillance, autonomous driving, and robotics. Accurately predicting object motion is fundamental to MOT, but current methods struggle with the complexities of real-world, non-linear motion (e.g., sudden stops, sharp turns). While recent research has gravitated towards increasingly complex and computationally expensive generative models to tackle this problem, their practical utility is often constrained. This paper challenges that paradigm, arguing that such complexity is not only unnecessary but can be outperformed by a more efficient, purpose-built approach. We introduce the Temporal Convolutional Motion Predictor (TCMP), a novel framework for MOT that leverages a modified Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) featuring dilated convolutions and a regression head. This design allows for effective motion prediction across arbitrary temporal context lengths. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, specifically improves upon the previous best method in several key metrics: HOTA (a measure of overall tracking accuracy) increases from 62.3% to 63.4%, IDF1 (a measure of identity preservation) rises from 63.0% to 65.0%, and AssA (a measure of association accuracy) improves from 47.2% to 49.1%. Significantly, TCMP achieves this performance while being highly efficient; it has only 0.014 times the parameters and requires only 0.05 times the computational cost (FLOPs) compared to the SOTA method. while is only 0.014 times the size (in terms of parameters) and requires only 0.05 times the computational cost (in terms of FLOPs). These findings highlight the robustness of our method to advance MOT systems by ensuring adaptability, accuracy, and efficiency in complex tracking environments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents TCMP, a lightweight Temporal Convolutional Network for motion prediction in multi-object tracking that outperforms complex generative models while being ~71x smaller and ~20x faster.
AI.motivation: Current MOT methods struggle with real-world non-linear motion and increasingly rely on complex, computationally expensive generative models whose practical utility is limited. The authors argue such complexity is unnecessary.
AI.method: TCMP uses a modified Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) with dilated convolutions and a regression head for effective motion prediction across arbitrary temporal context lengths.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA: HOTA 63.4% (+1.1%), IDF1 65.0% (+2.0%), AssA 49.1% (+1.9%) while having only 0.014x parameters and 0.05x FLOPs compared to prior SOTA.
AI.conclusion: A purpose-built, efficient approach can outperform complex generative models for motion prediction in MOT, demonstrating that simplicity and efficiency can advance tracking systems.

title: Flow matching for Sentinel-2 super-resolution: implementation, application, and implications
comment: 41 pages, 16 figures, 7 tables. Submitted in ISPRS Open Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
summary: Developing robust techniques for super-resolution of satellite imagery involves navigating commonly observed trade-offs between spectral fidelity and perceptual quality. In this work, we introduce a flow matching model for 4x super-resolution of 10-m Sentinel-2 visible and near-infrared bands over the conterminous United States (CONUS) using a dataset of 120,851 10-m Sentinel-2 and 2.5-m resampled NAIP imagery pairs acquired on the same day. Our results showed that the flow matching model outperformed diffusion and Real-ESRGAN models in pixel-wise accuracy in a single sampling step using the Euler method. When evaluated with a second-order Midpoint solver, our model generated perceptually realistic super-resolved imagery in only 20 sampling steps, effectively navigating the perception-distortion trade-off at inference time without retraining. We used this model to produce a super-resolved 2.5-m 4-band CONUS imagery product derived from 2025 10-m Sentinel-2 annual composites, consisting of over 1.58 trillion pixels. We further evaluated the use of super-resolved data on a land cover classification task using semantic segmentation models. Finally, we generated a yearly 2.5-m land cover product for the Chesapeake Bay watershed for 2020-2025. An accuracy assessment against 25,000 ground truth points revealed an overall accuracy of 89.11% for the annual land cover product. We conclude that flow matching is an effective generative modeling approach for super-resolution of Sentinel-2 imagery compared to diffusion and Generative Adversarial Network-based methods, and has strong implications for expanding access to high-resolution imagery for geospatial applications that demand fine spatial detail.

AI.tldr: A flow matching model achieves 4x super-resolution of Sentinel-2 imagery (10m→2.5m) over CONUS, outperforming diffusion and GAN models in perceptual quality and pixel accuracy, and is applied to large-scale land cover mapping with 89.11% classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: To overcome the spectral fidelity vs. perceptual quality trade-off in satellite image super-resolution, enabling high-resolution geospatial analysis without relying on costly high-resolution sensors.
AI.method: Flow matching model trained on 120,851 paired Sentinel-2 (10m) and NAIP (2.5m) images; uses Euler method for pixel-wise accuracy or Midpoint solver for perceptual realism; evaluated against diffusion and Real-ESRGAN baselines.
AI.result: Flow matching outperforms diffusion and Real-ESRGAN in pixel-wise accuracy (single Euler step); generates perceptually realistic results in 20 Midpoint steps; produces 1.58 trillion-pixel CONUS product; achieves 89.11% land cover accuracy over Chesapeake Bay.
AI.conclusion: Flow matching is an effective generative approach for satellite super-resolution, offering flexible perception-distortion trade-off at inference without retraining, and can democratize access to high-resolution imagery for geospatial applications.

title: RTPrune: Reading-Twice Inspired Token Pruning for Efficient DeepSeek-OCR Inference
summary: DeepSeek-OCR leverages visual-text compression to reduce long-text processing costs and accelerate inference, yet visual tokens remain prone to redundant textual and structural information. Moreover, current token pruning methods for conventional vision-language models (VLMs) fail to preserve textual fidelity due to improper compression mechanisms. By analyzing the decoding process of DeepSeek-OCR, we find that a distinct two-stage reading trajectory: the model initially prioritizes the majority of high-norm tokens, then subsequently redistributes its attention to the remaining ones. Motivated by this insight, we propose RTPrune, a two-stage token pruning method tailored for DeepSeek-OCR. In the first stage, we prioritize high-norm visual tokens that capture salient textual and structural information. In the second stage, the remaining tokens are paired and merged based on optimal transport theory to achieve efficient feature aggregation. We further introduce a dynamic pruning ratio that adapts to token similarity and textual density for OCR tasks, enabling a better efficiency-accuracy trade-off. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, as evidenced by 99.47% accuracy and 1.23$\times$ faster prefill on OmniDocBench, achieved with 84.25% token retention when applied to DeepSeek-OCR-Large.

AI.tldr: RTPrune is a two-stage token pruning method for DeepSeek-OCR that leverages visual-text compression. It achieves 99.47% accuracy with 1.23× faster prefill and 84.25% token retention by prioritizing high-norm tokens first, then merging remaining tokens via optimal transport, along with a dynamic pruning ratio for OCR tasks.
AI.motivation: DeepSeek-OCR's visual tokens contain redundant textual and structural information, and existing pruning methods for VLMs fail to preserve textual fidelity due to improper compression mechanisms.
AI.method: RTPrune has two stages: (1) prioritize high-norm visual tokens capturing salient information, (2) pair and merge remaining tokens using optimal transport theory. A dynamic pruning ratio adapts to token similarity and textual density.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results: 99.47% accuracy and 1.23× faster prefill on OmniDocBench with 84.25% token retention on DeepSeek-OCR-Large.
AI.conclusion: RTPrune effectively reduces token redundancy while preserving textual fidelity, achieving better efficiency-accuracy trade-offs for OCR tasks.

title: SIMON: Saliency-aware Integrative Multi-view Object-centric Neural Decoding
summary: Recent EEG-to-image retrieval methods leverage pretrained vision encoders and foveation-inspired priors, but typically assume a fixed, center-focused view. This center bias conflicts with content-driven human attention, creating a geometric-semantic dissociation between visual features and EEG responses. We propose SIMON, a saliency-aware multi-view framework for zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval. SIMON combines foreground segmentation and saliency prediction to select fixation centers via Saliency-Aware Sampling (SAS), then generates foveated views that emphasize informative object regions while suppressing background clutter. On THINGS-EEG, SIMON achieves state-of-the-art performance in both intra-subject and inter-subject settings, reaching an average Top-1 accuracy of 69.7% and 19.6%, respectively, consistently outperforming recent competitive baselines. Analyses across sampling granularity, EEG channel topology, and visual/brain encoder backbones further support the robustness of saliency-aware multi-view integration. Our code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/simonlink666/SIMON.

AI.tldr: SIMON is a saliency-aware multi-view framework for zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval that uses Saliency-Aware Sampling (SAS) to generate foveated views focusing on informative object regions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on THINGS-EEG (69.7% Top-1 intra-subject, 19.6% inter-subject).
AI.motivation: Existing EEG-to-image retrieval methods assume a fixed, center-focused view, creating a geometric-semantic dissociation between visual features and EEG responses, which conflicts with content-driven human attention.
AI.method: SIMON combines foreground segmentation and saliency prediction to select fixation centers via Saliency-Aware Sampling (SAS), then generates foveated views that emphasize informative object regions while suppressing background clutter.
AI.result: On THINGS-EEG, SIMON achieves state-of-the-art performance: 69.7% Top-1 accuracy (intra-subject) and 19.6% Top-1 accuracy (inter-subject), consistently outperforming recent competitive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Saliency-aware multi-view integration is robust across sampling granularity, EEG channel topology, and visual/brain encoder backbones, demonstrating the effectiveness of aligning visual foveation with human attention patterns for EEG-to-image retrieval.

title: BOLT: Online Lightweight Adaptation for Preparation-Free Heterogeneous Cooperative Perception
summary: Most existing heterogeneous cooperative perception methods depend on prior preparation like offline joint training or tailored collaborator-model adaptation. Such preprocessing is, however, generally impractical in real scenarios, as agents are usually independently trained by different developers and meet occasionally online. This work investigates \emph{preparation-free heterogeneous cooperative perception}, where agents use independently trained single-agent detectors without any pre-deployment coordination. We find direct cross-agent fusion under this setting greatly underperforms ego-only perception. We present BOLT, a lightweight plug-and-play module that adapts neighboring features online via ego-as-teacher distillation, requiring only ego predictions without ground-truth labels. BOLT leverages high-confidence ego perception features to guide cross-agent feature-domain alignment, while enabling neighbors to contribute features in the ego's low-confidence regions. With only 0.9M trainable parameters, BOLT improves AP@50 by up to 32.3 points over vanilla unadapted fusion in the preparation-free setting. It consistently outperforms ego-only results on DAIR-V2X and OPV2V, across different encoder pairs and fusion strategies. Code: https://github.com/sidiangongyuan/BOLT.

AI.tldr: BOLT is a lightweight plug-and-play module (0.9M parameters) that enables preparation-free heterogeneous cooperative perception by using ego-as-teacher distillation to adapt neighboring features online, improving AP@50 by up to 32.3 points over vanilla fusion and consistently outperforming ego-only perception.
AI.motivation: Existing heterogeneous cooperative perception methods require impractical offline preparation (joint training or model adaptation), but real-world agents are independently trained by different developers and meet online without coordination.
AI.method: BOLT is a plug-and-play module that uses ego-as-teacher distillation: ego's high-confidence perception features guide cross-agent feature alignment, while neighbors contribute features in ego's low-confidence regions. No ground-truth labels needed, only ego predictions.
AI.result: With only 0.9M trainable parameters, BOLT improves AP@50 by up to 32.3 points over vanilla unadapted fusion in preparation-free settings, and consistently outperforms ego-only perception on DAIR-V2X and OPV2V across different encoder pairs and fusion strategies.
AI.conclusion: BOLT effectively solves the practical challenge of preparation-free heterogeneous cooperative perception with a lightweight, plug-and-play solution that requires no pre-deployment coordination.

title: Beyond Heuristics: Learnable Density Control for 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary:
While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has demonstrated impressive real-time rendering performance, its efficacy remains constrained by a reliance on heuristic density control. Despite numerous refinements to these handcrafted rules, such methods inherently lack the flexibility to adapt to diverse scenes with complex geometries.
  In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift for density control from rigid heuristics to fully learnable policies. Specifically, we introduce \textbf{LeGS}, a framework that reformulates density control as a parameterized policy network optimized via Reinforcement Learning (RL). Central to our approach is the tailored effective reward function grounded in sensitivity analysis, which precisely quantifies the marginal contribution of individual Gaussians to reconstruction quality. To maintain computational tractability, we derive a closed-form solution that reduces the complexity of reward calculation from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N)$. Extensive experiments on the Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks \& Temples, and Deep Blending datasets demonstrate that \textbf{LeGS} significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, striking a superior balance between reconstruction quality and efficiency. The code will be released at https://github.com/AaronNZH/LeGS

AI.tldr: LeGS replaces heuristic density control in 3D Gaussian Splatting with a learnable policy network optimized via Reinforcement Learning, using a closed-form reward function based on sensitivity analysis. It achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: 3DGS relies on heuristic density control rules that lack flexibility to adapt to diverse scenes with complex geometries.
AI.method: Formulates density control as a parameterized policy network optimized via Reinforcement Learning, with a closed-form reward function (O(N) complexity) based on sensitivity analysis to quantify each Gaussian's marginal contribution.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks & Temples, and Deep Blending datasets, achieving a superior balance between reconstruction quality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Learnable density control via RL is a more flexible and effective paradigm than handcrafted heuristics for 3DGS.

title: LIMSSR: LLM-Driven Sequence-to-Score Reasoning under Training-Time Incomplete Multimodal Observations
summary: Real-world multimodal learning is often hindered by missing modalities. While Incomplete Multimodal Learning (IML) has gained traction, existing methods typically rely on the unrealistic assumption of full-modal availability during training to provide reconstruction supervision or cross-modal priors. This paper tackles the more challenging setting of IML under training-time incomplete observations, which precludes reliance on a ``God's eye view'' of complete data. We propose LIMSSR (LLM-Driven Incomplete Multimodal Sequence-to-Score Reasoning), a framework that reformulates this challenge as a conditional sequence reasoning task. LIMSSR leverages the semantic reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models via Prompt-Guided Context-Aware Modality Imputation and Multidimensional Representation Fusion to infer latent semantics from available contexts without direct reconstruction. To mitigate hallucinations, we introduce a Mask-Aware Dual-Path Aggregation to dynamically calibrate inference uncertainty. Extensive experiments on three Action Quality Assessment datasets demonstrate that LIMSSR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines without relying on complete training data, establishing a new paradigm for data-efficient multimodal learning. Code is available at https://github.com/XuHuangbiao/LIMSSR.

AI.tldr: LIMSSR is a framework for incomplete multimodal learning that uses LLMs for sequence-to-score reasoning, avoiding the need for complete training data by imputing latent semantics from available modalities through prompt-guided context-aware imputation and multidimensional fusion, with mask-aware dual-path aggregation to reduce hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Existing IML methods unrealistically assume full-modal availability during training; this work targets the realistic scenario where modality incompleteness persists in both training and inference.
AI.method: Reformulates IML as conditional sequence reasoning using LLMs, with Prompt-Guided Context-Aware Modality Imputation, Multidimensional Representation Fusion, and Mask-Aware Dual-Path Aggregation for hallucination mitigation.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on three Action Quality Assessment datasets without requiring complete training data.
AI.conclusion: LIMSSR establishes a new paradigm for data-efficient multimodal learning that works under realistic incomplete-data conditions both at training and inference time.

title: Scaling Video Understanding via Compact Latent Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) advance vision language understanding but face inherent limitations in long-video tasks due to bounded perception context budgets. Existing agentic methods mitigate this via rule-based preprocessing, yet often suffer from information loss, high cost, and reliance on textual intermediates. We propose MACF, an end-to-end Multi-Agent Collaboration Framework that decouples per-agent perception budgets from global video complexity, enabling scalable video understanding while preserving visual fidelity. MACF partitions videos into segments for locally budgeted agents and enables holistic reasoning via an agent-native latent communication protocol. Each agent encodes partial observations into compact, task-sufficient tokens in a shared embedding space, allowing efficient and information-preserving collaboration by a central coordinator. We introduce a curriculum training strategy that progressively enforces semantic alignment, evidence summarization, and cross-agent coordination. Extensive experiments on diverse video understanding benchmarks show that MACF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and multi-agent systems under identical budget constraints, demonstrating the effectiveness of our latent collaboration for scalable video understanding.

AI.tldr: MACF is an end-to-end multi-agent collaboration framework that decouples per-agent perception budgets from global video complexity, using latent communication protocols and curriculum training to enable scalable, information-preserving long-video understanding. It outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and multi-agent systems under identical budget constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs have bounded perception contexts that limit long-video understanding. Agentic methods using rule-based preprocessing cause information loss, high cost, and reliance on textual intermediates. The authors aim to overcome these limitations with an end-to-end latent collaboration approach.
AI.method: MACF partitions videos into segments, each handled by a locally budgeted agent. Agents encode observations into compact, task-sufficient tokens in a shared embedding space. A central coordinator enables holistic reasoning via an agent-native latent communication protocol. A curriculum training strategy progressively enforces semantic alignment, evidence summarization, and cross-agent coordination.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse video understanding benchmarks show MACF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and multi-agent systems under identical budget constraints.
AI.conclusion: The latent collaboration approach in MACF is effective for scalable video understanding, preserving visual fidelity while overcoming bounded perception budgets.

title: Learning from Compressed CT: Feature Attention Style Transfer and Structured Factorized Projections for Resource-Efficient Medical Image Analysis
summary: The deployment of artificial intelligence in medical imaging is hindered by high computational complexity and resource-intensive processing of volumetric data. Although chest computed tomography (CT) volumes offer richer diagnostic information than projection radiography, their use in AI-based diagnosis remains limited due to the computational burden of processing uncompressed volumetric images (typically stored in NIfTI or DICOM format). Addressing the growing need for low-resource deployment and efficient electronic data transfer, we investigate the utilization of JPEG-compressed chest CT volumes for thoracic abnormality detection. We propose Feature Attention Style Transfer (FAST), a novel distillation framework that transfers both activation patterns and structural relationships from high-fidelity CT representations to a spatiotemporal visual encoder operating on compressed inputs. By combining Gram-matrix-based attention style preservation with dual-attention feature alignment, FAST enables robust feature extraction from degraded volumes. Furthermore, we introduce Structured Factorized Projection (SFP), leveraging Block Tensor Train decomposition as a parameter-efficient alternative to dense projection layers, reducing projection-head parameters by almost half. Our contrastive learning pipeline, CT-Lite, integrates these components with a SigLIP-based multimodal alignment objective. Experiments on CT-RATE, NIDCH, and Rad-ChestCT demonstrate that CT-Lite achieves AUROC within 5-7\% of the uncompressed-input baseline across all three datasets, despite operating on compressed inputs with significantly fewer parameters, paving the way for AI-based clinical evaluation under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CT-Lite, a framework combining Feature Attention Style Transfer (FAST) and Structured Factorized Projection (SFP) to enable efficient thoracic abnormality detection from JPEG-compressed chest CT volumes, achieving AUROC within 5-7% of uncompressed baselines with significantly fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: AI deployment in medical imaging is limited by high computational complexity and resource-intensive processing of volumetric data. Chest CT volumes offer richer diagnostic information than radiography but remain underutilized in AI diagnosis due to computational burden of uncompressed formats, motivating the need for low-resource and efficient data transfer solutions.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) FAST (Feature Attention Style Transfer) - a distillation framework transferring activation patterns and structural relationships from high-fidelity CT to a spatiotemporal encoder on compressed inputs using Gram-matrix attention style preservation and dual-attention feature alignment; (2) SFP (Structured Factorized Projection) using Block Tensor Train decomposition to reduce projection-head parameters by half; all integrated into a SigLIP-based contrastive learning pipeline called CT-Lite.
AI.result: On three datasets (CT-RATE, NIDCH, Rad-ChestCT), CT-Lite achieves AUROC within 5-7% of the uncompressed-input baseline while operating on compressed inputs with significantly fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates feasibility of AI-based clinical evaluation under resource constraints by enabling efficient processing of compressed CT volumes, potentially expanding deployment of AI diagnostics in low-resource settings.

title: From Local to Global to Mechanistic: An iERF-Centered Unified Framework for Interpreting Vision Models
comment: Accepted to IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI), 2026
summary: Modern vision models achieve remarkable accuracy, but explaining where evidence arises, what the model encodes, and how internal computations assemble that evidence remains fragmented. We introduce an iERF-centric framework that unifies local, global, and mechanistic interpretability around a single analysis unit: the pointwise feature vector (PFV) paired with its instance-specific Effective Receptive Field (iERF). On the local side, Sharing Ratio Decomposition (SRD) expresses each PFV as a mixture of upstream PFVs via sharing ratios and propagates iERFs to construct class-discriminative saliency maps. SRD yields high-resolution, activation-faithful explanations, is robust to targeted manipulation and noise, and remains activation-agnostic across common nonlinearities. For the global view, we introduce Concept-Anchored Feature Explanation (CAFE), which utilizes the iERF as a semantic label, grounding abstract latent vectors in verifiable pixel-level evidence. With CAFE, we address the challenge of non-localized sparse autoencoder latents--especially in Transformers, where early self-attention mixes distant context. To answer how representations are composed through depth, we propose the Interlayer Concept Graph with Interlayer Concept Attribution (ICAT), which quantifies concept-to-concept influence while isolating layer pairs; an interlayer insertion, deletion protocol identifies Integrated Gradients as the most faithful instantiation. Empirically, across ResNet50, VGG16, and ViTs, our framework outperforms baselines in both fidelity and robustness, successfully interprets dispersed SAE features, and exposes dominant concept routes in correct, misclassified, and adversarial cases. Grounded in iERFs, our approach provides a coherent, evidence-backed map from pixels to concepts to decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an iERF-centric framework unifying local, global, and mechanistic interpretability by using pointwise feature vectors paired with instance-specific Effective Receptive Fields. It proposes SRD for local saliency maps, CAFE for global concept grounding, and ICAT for analyzing interlayer concept composition, demonstrating strong fidelity and robustness across ResNet50, VGG16, and ViTs.
AI.motivation: Modern vision models achieve high accuracy but lack a unified understanding of where evidence arises, what the model encodes, and how internal computations assemble that evidence. Existing interpretability methods are fragmented across local, global, and mechanistic levels.
AI.method: The framework uses iERF (instance-specific Effective Receptive Field) as a core analysis unit. It proposes: (1) SRD (Sharing Ratio Decomposition) for local saliency maps via propagating iERFs, (2) CAFE (Concept-Anchored Feature Explanation) for grounding latent vectors in pixel evidence, and (3) ICAT (Interlayer Concept Attribution) with an insertion/deletion protocol for quantifying concept-to-concept influence.
AI.result: The framework outperforms baselines in fidelity and robustness across ResNet50, VGG16, and ViTs. It successfully interprets dispersed SAE features and exposes dominant concept routes in correct, misclassified, and adversarial cases.
AI.conclusion: Grounded in iERFs, the approach provides a coherent, evidence-backed map from pixels to concepts to decisions, unifying local, global, and mechanistic interpretability into a single framework.

title: Leveraging Vision-Language Models as Weak Annotators in Active Learning
summary: Active learning aims to reduce annotation cost by selectively querying informative samples for supervision under a limited labeling budget. In this work, we investigate how vision-language models (VLMs) can be leveraged to further reduce the reliance on costly human annotation within the active learning paradigm. To this end, we find that the reliability of VLMs varies significantly with label granularity in fine-grained recognition tasks: they perform poorly on fine-grained labels but can provide accurate coarse-grained labels.
